content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Supply-side contraction of the selective track (shrink the gate, not the rules)'. Hungary's early-selection system at ages 10–12 increases achievement inequality and socio-economic stratification without raising average performance, and similar learning gains to Portugal's are achievable through non-structural reforms (curriculum clarity, assessment transparency, retention reduction, targeted support) within a comprehensive framework.
opportunities: Shrinks the selective track's footprint without requiring statutory redefinition of the secondary-education age boundary, potentially avoiding the felmenő-rendszer and ~1-year lead-time obligations that a structural reform would trigger.

Aligns policy with the demographic reality that the exam's original rationing function is eroding, as evidenced by falling 8-year-track applications (-14% year-on-year) alongside flat overall enrolment.
strengths: Portugal maintained a comprehensive (non-selective) school structure through compulsory basic education and improved PISA results by approximately 31 points (0.3 standard deviations) between 2000 and 2015–2018 without introducing early tracking.

Portugal's documented gains in learning outcomes resulted from curriculum standards clarification, increased school autonomy, regular student assessment, and vocational pathway development—not from changes to selection age.
threats: Reversal requires legislative repeal since score thresholds are maintainer-level administrative practice rather than statute, offering more procedural friction than S1's decree-based mechanism, but a parliamentary majority change during the multi-year rollout still poses a significant reversal risk given the Fidesz framing of any selection-related change and the government's own pattern of rapidly reversing predecessor reforms.

Church and foundation maintainers, protected by Tisza's coalition commitment to preserve prior operating conditions, may simply expand to absorb displaced demand, neutralizing the intended contraction and creating a policy-credibility problem.
weaknesses: Whether targeted support (TEIP-type) can close achievement gaps rather than merely reducing dropout rates remains uncertain because no longitudinal study has tracked student competency trajectories beyond retention reduction.

The fiscal and pedagogical cost of phase-out or structural reform of Hungary's 8-year grammar school track (replacing it with comprehensive entry and specialist bands at 14+) has not been modeled in published Hungarian research.
